Q1: I tried to carve out consistent blocks of time for studying, usually around 26 hours a week spread across evenings and weekends since I work during the day. Being 29 and juggling multiple responsibilities, I had to be intentional about when I studied. I'm a visual learner, so I'd often organize my materials with color-coded notes and diagrams. My routine wasn't perfect, but I did my best to stay on track despite everything else going on in my life.
Q2: Honestly, my pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was due and what was happening at work. I'd try to maintain some regularity, but deadlines definitely pushed me into more intense study sessions. Some weeks I'd be consistent, other weeks I'd have to cram everything into a few days. Being enrolled in nine online courses meant I was constantly juggling priorities and adjusting my schedule.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew an assignment was coming up or when I needed to check on deadlines. Sometimes I'd get an email notification that would prompt me to check in. I'll admit my attendance wasn't great at 32%, but when I did log in, it was purposeful. I'd also log in to watch video lectures since those really helped me understand the material visually.
Q4: There were stretches where logging in became more habitual, especially when I was actively working on assignments. I'd check the discussion boards and watch videos as part of my evening wind-down routine. But I'll be honest, it never became as automatic as I wanted it to be. With nine courses and limited resources, the LMS sometimes felt like one more thing competing for my attention.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best visually. I spent a lot of time watching those—over two hours worth—because seeing concepts explained helped them stick. The discussion posts were also valuable because I could learn from other students' perspectives. I participated in about 19 discussions and had 25 peer interactions, which helped me feel connected to the material and the community.
Q6: I tended to skip some of the reading-heavy resources because they didn't match my learning style as well. If something was text-dense without visual aids, I'd often put it off or skim through it quickly. Some optional materials I ignored entirely because I was already stretched thin across multiple courses. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what would give me the most return on investment.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. If it helped me visualize a concept or gave me practical skills I could apply, I was all in. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because that collaborative element made learning feel less isolating. Basically, if I could see how it would help me succeed, I engaged with it fully.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford. Required ones got my attention because they directly impacted my grades, but optional ones would slip down my priority list. That said, when I did engage with optional content, it often felt more relaxed and exploratory. I didn't have the same pressure, so I could actually enjoy learning for its own sake in those moments.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos to study when I didn't have reliable internet or when I was commuting. I also used external YouTube tutorials and free resources I found online since my budget is tight. My handwritten notes and visual diagrams were a big part of how I processed information, and none of that showed up in the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems in a notebook, creating mind maps, or reviewing printed materials without ever touching the LMS. I'd sometimes spend hours studying from downloaded content or discussing concepts with a study buddy in person. The LMS would show me as inactive, but I was actually deeply engaged with the course material. My offline study time was probably equal to or greater than my online time.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd access a video lecture or PDF once, download it, and then work with it offline multiple times. Given my limited resources and sometimes spotty internet, this was the most practical way to study. I'd revisit downloaded materials repeatedly while the LMS only recorded that single initial access. It was efficient for me but probably made my engagement look lower than it actually was.
Q12: My anxiety really spiked at certain points, especially around exam time, which made it harder to focus and engage. Even though I'm generally resilient, there were moments when the weight of nine courses felt overwhelming. Work pressures and financial stress also competed for my mental energy. During those times, I'd still push through because that's what I do, but the quality of my engagement definitely suffered.
Q13: Definitely. When work got demanding or I was exhausted, my LMS usage dropped significantly. Confusion with certain course materials would sometimes make me avoid logging in because I felt anxious about falling behind. I'd compensate by studying offline where I felt more in control. The competing responsibilities of being a 29-year-old with limited resources meant the LMS often took a backseat to more immediate concerns.
Q14: There were times I'd log in and scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—worried about bills or work issues—and I'd just be going through the motions. I might watch a video but realize afterward I hadn't retained much. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they'd show up as engagement time in the system.
Q15: I definitely postponed studying for exams more than I should have, which probably contributed to my low exam score. The anxiety around high-stakes assessments made me avoid them until the last minute. I'd focus on assignments instead because I felt more confident about those and could control the outcome better. It's something I recognized and tried to address through the CBT sessions, which actually helped my mood quite a bit.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary driver for LMS engagement. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, cramming in video watching and assignment work. My task completion rate of 85% shows I did get things done, but it was often last-minute. Without deadlines, I probably would have been even less consistent with logging in. They provided structure I needed but also added to my anxiety.
Q17: When I received positive feedback on assignments, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. My assignment scores were decent—around 80%—so that encouragement kept me going. Negative or critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me hesitant to engage for a bit. Overall, feedback helped me understand what was working, but the emotional impact varied depending on my mental state at the time.
Q18: The course structure with its multiple components gave me flexibility but also required a lot of self-direction. Having video content available was great for my visual learning style. However, managing nine courses simultaneously meant I couldn't give each one the attention its structure demanded. I participated more when the structure included discussion elements because that peer interaction kept me accountable.
Q19: The video content made engagement much easier for someone like me who learns visually. Having materials available for download was also crucial given my situation. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on text-based content or had confusing navigation. Also, when course expectations weren't clear, my anxiety would spike and I'd disengage until I could figure things out.
Q20: I think that could reflect someone who's genuinely trying to learn but struggling with the assessment format. They might be engaging with content to understand it but then freezing up when it comes to producing work. Or they could be dealing with anxiety or perfectionism that makes submission feel terrifying. Sometimes high activity is actually someone spinning their wheels, trying to understand something that just isn't clicking.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience to some degree. My LMS activity was relatively low, but I was studying extensively offline. Someone might download materials once and study them for weeks without logging back in. Or they might have strong prior knowledge that means they don't need to engage as much with the LMS content. The platform doesn't capture independent learning or real-world application of knowledge.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and completed tasks—the mechanical aspects of engagement. They capture patterns like login frequency and time spent on videos. But they miss the quality of that engagement, whether someone was actually learning or just present. They also miss all offline studying, emotional state during learning, and whether the student was genuinely connecting with the material intellectually.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively working to understand and apply what I'm learning. It's not just logging in or watching videos—it's asking questions, making connections, and caring about the outcome. For me, engagement includes the offline work: the diagrams I draw, the problems I work through, the discussions with peers outside the platform. True engagement is when learning feels meaningful, not just mandatory.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I grew through this course despite my struggles, or how the CBT sessions helped me manage my anxiety around academics. It wouldn't capture my resilience—how I kept pushing forward despite limited resources and a demanding schedule. My offline study habits, my visual learning strategies, and the peer support I found outside the platform are all invisible. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite failing the exam, I'm still here, still trying, and still learning.
